_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Greene County, OH. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Greene County was formed in 1803 from Hamilton and Ross counties, with Xenia as county seat. Located squarely in the Virginia Military District, the county holds some of the best-preserved early Ohio records, with continuous series from formation and no major courthouse fire.

previous_courthouses.location: Xenia, brick courthouse on Detroit Street
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced with larger building. Records survived.

previous_courthouses.location: Xenia, enlarged courthouse on same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Served until replaced by the 1901 building. Records survived transfer.

records_destroyed: some loose papers in auxiliary storage
record_losses.scope: Minor — courthouse sustained structural damage but bound records in vaults survived
record_losses.description: The April 3, 1974, F5 tornado that struck Xenia as part of the Super Outbreak was the most destructive tornado in the city's history, killing 33 people and destroying much of downtown. The courthouse sustained structural damage but the record vaults held. Some loose papers in auxiliary storage were lost or water-damaged. Bound volumes — deed books, probate journals, marriage registers — survived intact. The tornado also destroyed private records in hundreds of homes across Xenia.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s family was in Xenia in April 1974, the tornado is the dividing line of memory. The county records survived, but the family photographs, letters, and personal papers in the destroyed homes did not. The 1974 tornado is not ancient history — living descendants remember it.
record_losses.source: Schmidlin, Thomas W., and Jeanne Appelhans Schmidlin. Thunder in the Heartland: A Chronicle of Outstanding Weather Events in Ohio. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1996.

record_losses.description: Greene County has experienced no courthouse fire. Records run in continuous series from the 1803 formation. The county's position in the Virginia Military District means early land records are unusually rich — the metes-and-bounds surveys, warrant transfers, and patent records document the original settlement in detail unavailable in surveyed-grid counties.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Greene County's unbroken records from 1803 mean that if {ancestor_name} appears in this county, the documentation almost certainly survives. The Virginia Military District land records add a layer of detail — warrant numbers, original patentees, chain of title — that rectangular-survey counties cannot match.
record_losses.source: Robinson, George F. History of Greene County, Ohio. Chicago: S. J. Clarke, 1902.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county organization. Virginia Military District land records add warrant entries, surveys, and patents.
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. VMD metes-and-bounds surveys create complex but well-documented land chains.
deeds.repository: Greene County Recorder, Xenia; Ohio History Connection microfilm; Ohio Land Office records (Ohio History Connection)
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com OH land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — probate records from county organization. Estate inventories are detailed and well-preserved.
wills_estates.gaps: Early loose papers fragile but legible.
wills_estates.repository: Greene County Probate Court, Xenia; Ohio History Connection

marriages.completeness: Excellent — marriage records from county organization
marriages.gaps: Some frontier-period marriages may lack licenses. After 1850, records are comprehensive.
marriages.repository: Greene County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — Court of Common Pleas minutes from organization
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records incomplete before 1830.
court_minutes.repository: Greene County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio statewide registration began 1908. Compliance spotty in rural townships until mid-1910s. Some probate court birth/death records from the 1860s-1907 under earlier Ohio statutes.
vital_records.repository: Greene County Probate Court; Ohio Department of Health; Greene County Room at Greene County Public Library

substitute_sources.source_type: Virginia Military District warrant and patent records
substitute_sources.description: The Ohio Land Office maintained records of all Virginia Military District warrant entries, surveys, and patents. These records document the original warrant holder (often a Virginia Revolutionary War veteran), any assignees, the survey description, and the final patent. Greene County falls entirely within the VMD.
substitute_sources.repository: Ohio History Connection (Ohio Land Office records); Bureau of Land Management General Land Office records; FamilySearch microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: VMD records connect Ohio settlers to Virginia military service. The warrant chain — from the original veteran through assignees to the final patentee — reveals kinship and economic networks. A warrant that passed through three hands before reaching Greene County tells a story of debt, speculation, or family inheritance.
substitute_sources.source: Peters, William E. Ohio Lands and Their Subdivision. Athens: Ohio University Press, 1918.

substitute_sources.source_type: Wilberforce University and Central State University records
substitute_sources.description: Wilberforce University (founded 1856, the oldest private historically Black university in the US) and Central State University (founded 1887) are both located in Greene County near Xenia. University records — enrollment lists, alumni records, faculty records — document the Black community in Greene County and across the nation.
substitute_sources.repository: Wilberforce University Archives; Central State University Archives; Ohio History Connection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Enrollment records at Wilberforce and Central State document Black families who sent children to these institutions from across the country. Alumni directories provide post-graduation addresses and occupations. Faculty records document the Black professional class in Greene County.
substitute_sources.source: McGinnis, Frederick A. A History and an Interpretation of Wilberforce University. Blanchester, OH: Brown Publishing, 1941.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800–1940. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) are particularly valuable for Greene County's farming economy.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. The 1850–1880 agricultural schedules document farm operations in detail — acreage, crops, livestock, value of farm machinery.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Greene County, OH.

narrative_hooks: Greene County's records run from 1803 without a break — no fire, no flood, no war damage. If {ancestor_name} left a mark in this county, the paper trail survived.

The Virginia Military District land records in Greene County trace back to a Revolutionary War bounty warrant — {ancestor_name}'s land title may link directly to a soldier who fought at Yorktown or Valley Forge.
The 1974 tornado that flattened Xenia killed thirty-three people and destroyed half the downtown — the courthouse vaults held, but the family papers in {ancestor_name}'s home did not.

If {ancestor_name} was Black and connected to Wilberforce University, the university archives may document {pronoun_possessive} family more thoroughly than county records — enrollment lists, alumni records, and campus publications that recorded names the courthouse may not have.
source_references: Robinson, George F. History of Greene County, Ohio. Chicago: S. J. Clarke, 1902.
Peters, William E. Ohio Lands and Their Subdivision. Athens: Ohio University Press, 1918.
Schmidlin, Thomas W., and Jeanne Appelhans Schmidlin. Thunder in the Heartland: A Chronicle of Outstanding Weather Events in Ohio. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1996.
McGinnis, Frederick A. A History and an Interpretation of Wilberforce University. Blanchester, OH: Brown Publishing, 1941.
